nativeRange.habitat: The Amano Shrimp is native to freshwater streams and rivers with rocky substrates, typically shaded by overhanging vegetation. Unusually for a freshwater shrimp, the larvae drift downstream and require low-salinity brackish water to complete their early development before migrating back upstream as juveniles.

decorPreferences: driftwood and leaf litter for biofilm grazing
rocks and caves for molting shelter

diet.notes: Generally considered one of the most effective algae-grazing freshwater shrimp, the Amano Shrimp readily consumes soft green algae, biofilm, and decaying plant matter. It will also accept blanched vegetables such as zucchini, spinach, and cucumber, along with sinking shrimp pellets, algae wafers, and most prepared sinking fish foods. Including calcium-rich foods in the diet is generally recommended to support healthy moulting.

avoidWith: any fish large enough to swallow an adult shrimp

summary: The Amano Shrimp (Caridina multidentata) is widely regarded as one of the most effective algae-grazing shrimp in the freshwater hobby, growing to around 4–5 cm. Native to streams in Japan, Taiwan, and Korea, adults thrive in pure freshwater, but their larvae require brackish water to develop — meaning the species rarely reproduces in home aquariums.
careNotes: Amano Shrimp are generally considered one of the most beginner-friendly freshwater shrimp species. They tolerate a wide range of water parameters but appreciate stable, well-cycled tanks with moderate hardness to support successful moulting. A tightly-fitting lid is strongly recommended — they are well-documented escape artists, particularly during the night or after water changes, and can climb filter intakes, heater cords, and air-line tubing out of the tank. Like all invertebrates, they are highly copper-sensitive; avoid copper-based medications and check that any plant fertilisers used do not contain elevated copper. Often described as gregarious — keeping a small group of three or more reduces stress and dominance behaviour at feeding time. Note that imported animals frequently arrive translucent or pale and can take several weeks to settle into their normal pigmentation.
breedingNotes: Generally considered impractical in standard home aquaria. Although adult females readily carry eggs in freshwater, the hatched larvae require brackish water (around SG 1.020–1.024) to develop through several planktonic zoeal stages before metamorphosing into juveniles and migrating back to freshwater. This amphidromous life cycle means that without a dedicated brackish larval-rearing setup and gradual re-acclimation protocol, larvae die within days. Distinct from Neocaridina and the Caridina cantonensis complex, which both breed freely in pure freshwater.

additional.name: The Shrimp Farm
additional.notes: Designated secondary returned HTTP 403 on access; could not be cross-checked. Listed for traceability; swapped to Flip Aquatics and Fishkeeping World as substitute secondaries.

additional.name: Flip Aquatics
additional.notes: Fallback secondary used in place of blocked Shrimp Farm page. Provided water-parameter ranges (pH 7.0–7.6, GH 7–15, KH 2–8) and tank-size guidance (4 gal minimum, 20 gal recommended).

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Third cross-check source. Confirmed family Atyidae, adult size up to 2 in (~5 cm), lifespan 2–3 years, brackish larval requirement, and recommended group size of six or more.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Healthy adults generally moult roughly every 3–6 weeks; juveniles moult more frequently. Shed exoskeletons should be left in the tank as a calcium source. Sudden parameter swings (especially large water changes with very different GH) are commonly associated with failed moults.
crustacean.breedingNotes: The Amano Shrimp is amphidromous, meaning larvae must migrate to brackish water to survive and complete their development, then return to freshwater as juveniles. They require salinity around SG 1.020–1.024 during this larval stage. Without a dedicated brackish rearing setup and a gradual re-acclimation protocol, reproduction is effectively impossible in a standard freshwater aquarium.
